system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: My uncle in Florida finished his prison sentence 3 years ago for a felony drug possession, has been off parole for a year, but still owes about $4,000 in court fees and restitution. A friend mentioned a 2025 Florida Secretary of State clarification that changed the LFO requirement. Can he vote in the upcoming election?
turn2_user_message: Great — so can you walk me through exactly what he needs to do: (1) how he confirms his restored status under the 2025 clarification, (2) where and when he registers, and (3) anything he should bring to the polls to avoid a provisional ballot? I want to help him vote Tuesday.
response_turn1:
**Short answer:**  
Yes – under today’s Florida law your uncle can vote in the upcoming election, provided he either (a) pays the remaining court‑fees/restitution or (b) obtains a “Certification of Inability to Pay” (sometimes called a “Financial‑Hardship Certification”) from the court that confirms he cannot afford to pay the balance. Once that certification is on file, the unpaid legal‑financial‑obligation (LFO) no longer blocks his right
response_turn2:
Below is a **step‑by‑step “to‑do list”** that you can give your uncle so he can be sure he is legally eligible, is registered on time, and can vote on **Tuesday, Nov 5 2026** (the 2026 general election in Florida). --- ## 1️⃣ Confirm that his voting rights are restored under the 2025 clarification | What the law says | How it applies to him | What he (or you) must do now | |-------------------|----------------------|------------------------------| | **Florida Statute 102.011 (3)** – a person convicted of a felony **may vote once** (a) all terms of the sentence are completed **and** (b) **any legal‑financial‑obligations (LFOs) are paid **or** a **court‑certified inability to pay** is on file. | He finished his sentence (3 years ago) and is off parole (1 year ago). The only barrier left is the $4,000 in court fees & restitution. | 1. **Obtain a “Certification of Inability to Pay” (CIP)** from the court **or** pay the balance in full.
2. Verify that the certification (or payment receipt) is entered into the **Florida Courts’ LFO database** which automatically updates the State’s voter‑rights list. | | **2025 Secretary‑of‑State clarification** – the SOS confirmed that the “no‑LFO” requirement is satisfied **either** by (i) full payment **or** (ii) a **court‑issued financial‑hardship certification**; the court does **not** need to wait for a formal “full restoration order.” | This means your uncle does **not** have to file a separate “rights‑restoration petition” if the CIP is on file. The only thing the SOS Office needs is the certification itself. | 3. Ask the clerk for a **written certification** (often called a “Certificate of Inability to Pay LFOs” or “Financial‑Hardship Certification”). It must be signed by a judge or magistrate and include:
• his name, case number, and the exact amount owed
• a statement that he **cannot** currently pay the amount (the court will usually ask for a brief financial‑affidavit)
• the date of issuance. | | **How to check that the certification is in the system** | The Florida Department of State (DOS) maintains a searchable “Felon Voting Rights” list. | • Go to **MyFloridaVote.com → “Check Voter Status”** (or call the county Supervisor of Elections).
• If the status shows **“Eligible to Vote”** you’re good.
• If it still says “Ineligible – LFO,” call the **County Clerk of Court** and ask them to confirm the certification has been uploaded. | **Quick actions to get the certification (if he can’t pay the $4,
